mnemonics.title: Dual Mode Key Points
mnemonics.content: U-GOT-K: User (limited), Got (system call), Operation requires, Transition to Kernel Mode - only Kernel has full access

mnemonics.title: Privileged Instructions
mnemonics.content: P-I-E: Privileged Instructions need Kernel (Execute in supervisor mode only)

mnemonics.title: Mode Switching Triggers
mnemonics.content: S-I-T: System call, Interrupt, Trap - all switch to Kernel Mode

acronyms.expansion: Operating System - manages hardware and provides services
acronyms.usage: The OS runs in Kernel Mode while user apps run in User Mode
CPU has mode bit to distinguish between User and Kernel Modes

acronyms.expansion: Input/Output - data transfer between CPU and devices
acronyms.usage: I/O operations are privileged and require Kernel Mode

acronyms.expansion: Memory Management Unit - handles memory access
acronyms.usage: MMU enforces memory protection in User Mode
System calls provide API for user programs to request OS services

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a building with two areas: a public lobby (User Mode) where visitors can do basic things, and a secure control room (Kernel Mode) where only authorized staff (OS) can access critical systems. A receptionist (system call) manages requests.

Picture concentric circles with Ring 0 as the innermost circle (highest privilege, center), expanding outward to Ring 3 as the outermost circle (lowest privilege). Only inner circles can access outer areas.

Think of a toll booth on a highway: user programs drive on the user road, but when they need services (like crossing a bridge), they must stop at the toll booth (system call/trap) where the operator (kernel) handles their request, then they continue.

analogies.analogy: Like a bank: customers (User Mode) can do basic transactions at counters but cannot access the vault (Kernel Mode). Only authorized bank staff can access the vault, and every access is logged.
Like ordering food in a restaurant: you (user process) cannot go into the kitchen (kernel). You give your order to a waiter (system call), who brings your food from the kitchen back to you.
Like a hotel key card: regular guests (User Mode) can only access their room floor, but hotel staff (Kernel Mode) have master keys that can access all areas including server rooms.

Like a security clearance badge: when you wear the visitor badge (mode bit = 0, User Mode), doors with restricted access remain locked. When you wear the admin badge (mode bit = 1, Kernel Mode), all doors open.
